title.zh: 卷四十三 酈陸朱劉叔孫傳
title.en: Volume 43: Li, Lu, Zhu, Liu and Shusun
title.raw: 卷四十三 酈陸朱劉叔孫傳 Volume 43: Li, Lu, Zhu, Liu and Shusun

sentences.zh: 卷四十三 酈陸硃劉叔孫傳第十三

translations.literal: Scroll 43: Biographies of Li, Lu, Zhu, Liu, and Shusun, Part 13
translations.idiomatic: Scroll 43: Biographies of Li Shiqi, Lu Jia, Zhu Jian, Liu Jing, and Shusun Tong, Part 13

sentences.zh: 酈食其

translations.literal: Li Shiqi
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi

sentences.zh: 酈食其，陳留高陽人也。

translations.literal: Li Shiqi was a man of Gaoyang in Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi came from Gaoyang in Chenliu.

sentences.zh: 好讀書，家貧落魄，無衣食業。

translations.literal: He loved to read books; his family was poor and he was down on his luck, with no livelihood for food or clothing.
translations.idiomatic: He loved books, but his household was poor and he drifted without prospects, with no trade to keep him fed or clothed.

sentences.zh: 為里監門，然吏縣中賢豪不敢役，皆謂之狂生。

translations.literal: He served as gatekeeper for the village, yet the clerks and the worthy and powerful men of the county did not dare to make him do labor; they all called him the wild scholar.
translations.idiomatic: He kept watch at the village gate, yet clerks and local notables in the county never dared press him into corvée duty; they all called him a madcap scholar.

sentences.zh: 及陳胜、項梁等起，諸將徇地過高陽者數十人，食其聞其將皆握齪好荷禮自用，不能听大度之言，食其乃自匿。

translations.literal: When Chen Sheng, Xiang Liang, and others rose, several dozen generals who were seizing territory passed through Gaoyang; when Li Shiqi heard that their commanders were all petty, fond of ceremony, and self-willed, and could not heed words of great vision, Li Shiqi therefore hid himself.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Sheng and Xiang Liang rose in revolt, dozens of generals on campaign passed through Gaoyang. Li Shiqi found their leaders narrow-minded, sticklers for empty ritual, and deaf to large-minded counsel, so he kept out of sight.

sentences.zh: 后聞沛公略地陳留郊，沛公麾下騎士适食其里中子，沛公時時問邑中賢豪。

translations.literal: Later he heard that the Lord of Pei was taking the outskirts of Chenliu, and that a mounted man in the Lord of Pei's train was a fellow villager of Li Shiqi's; the Lord of Pei often asked about the worthy and powerful men of the town.
translations.idiomatic: Later he learned that the Lord of Pei was advancing through the Chenliu suburbs, and that one of the Lord of Pei's horsemen was a neighbor from his hamlet—the Lord of Pei was forever asking who the local worthies were.

sentences.zh: 騎士歸，食其見，謂曰：「吾聞沛公嫚易人，有大略，此真吾所愿從游，莫為我先。

translations.literal: When the horseman returned, Li Shiqi saw him and said, "I have heard that the Lord of Pei treats people brusquely but has great strategic vision; he is truly the one I wish to follow and roam with—do not fail to speak for me first.
translations.idiomatic: When the rider came home, Li Shiqi waylaid him and said, "They say the Lord of Pei is rough with people but thinks in broad strokes; he is exactly the patron I want to attach myself to—please put in a word for me before anyone else.

sentences.zh: 若見沛公，謂曰『臣里中有酈生，年六十餘，長八尺，人皆謂之狂生，自謂我非狂。』」

translations.literal: If you see the Lord of Pei, tell him, 'In my hamlet there is a Li Sheng, over sixty years old, eight feet tall; people all call him the wild scholar, but he says of himself that he is not wild.'"
translations.idiomatic: If you see him, say: "There is a Li Sheng in our hamlet—past sixty, eight feet tall; men call him a madcap, though he insists he is not."'

sentences.zh: 騎士曰：「沛公不喜儒，諸客冠儒冠來者，沛公輒解其冠，溺其中。

translations.literal: The horseman said, "The Lord of Pei does not like Confucians; when guests come wearing Confucian caps, the Lord of Pei always pulls off their caps and urinates in them.
translations.idiomatic: The rider warned him: "The Lord of Pei despises scholars. When visitors arrive in Confucian caps, he snatches the cap off and relieves himself in it.

sentences.zh: 与人言，常大罵。

translations.literal: When he speaks with people, he often reviles them loudly.
translations.idiomatic: In conversation he often bellows abuse.

sentences.zh: 未可以儒生說也。」

translations.literal: You cannot yet persuade him with the talk of a Confucian."
translations.idiomatic: A scholar's pitch will not work on him yet."

sentences.zh: 食其曰：「第言之。」

translations.literal: Li Shiqi said, "Just say it as I told you."
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi said, "Say it anyway, exactly as I said."

sentences.zh: 騎士從容言食其所戒者。

translations.literal: The horseman casually repeated to the Lord of Pei the points Li Shiqi had charged him with.
translations.idiomatic: At his leisure the rider passed on to the Lord of Pei what Li Shiqi had asked him to say.

sentences.zh: 沛公至高陽傳舍，使人召食其。

translations.literal: When the Lord of Pei reached the relay inn at Gaoyang, he sent for Li Shiqi.
translations.idiomatic: When the Lord of Pei reached the post station at Gaoyang, he sent for Li Shiqi.

sentences.zh: 食其至，入謁，沛公方踞床令兩女子洗，而見食其。

translations.literal: When Li Shiqi arrived, he entered to pay his respects; the Lord of Pei was just then sitting with legs sprawled on the couch, having two women wash his feet, while he received Li Shiqi.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi came in to pay his respects. The Lord of Pei was sprawling on a couch while two women washed his feet, and he received Li Shiqi like that.

sentences.zh: 食其入，即長揖不拜，曰：「足下欲助秦攻諸侯乎？

translations.literal: When Li Shiqi entered, he at once made a long bow without the full prostration and said, "Do you, sir, wish to help Qin attack the feudal lords?
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi stepped in, offered a deep bow without full kneeling, and said, "Sir, do you mean to aid Qin against the feudal lords?

sentences.zh: 欲率諸侯破秦乎？」

translations.literal: Or do you wish to lead the feudal lords to destroy Qin?"
translations.idiomatic: Or do you mean to lead the lords in smashing Qin?"

sentences.zh: 沛公罵曰：「豎儒！

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei cursed, "Confucian pedant!
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei snarled, "You pedantic fool!

sentences.zh: 夫天下同苦秦久矣，故諸侯相率攻秦，何謂助秦？」

translations.literal: All under Heaven have long suffered together under Qin; therefore the feudal lords have joined in attacking Qin—what do you mean by helping Qin?"
translations.idiomatic: The empire has groaned under Qin long enough; that is why the lords are banding together to strike Qin—how is that 'helping Qin'?"

sentences.zh: 食其曰：「必欲聚徒合義兵誅無道秦，不宜踞見長者。」

translations.literal: Li Shiqi said, "If you truly wish to gather followers and unite a righteous host to punish the lawless Qin, you ought not to receive an elder while sitting with legs sprawled like this."
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi replied, "If you truly mean to rally men and righteous arms to bring down lawless Qin, you should not meet an older man sprawled on a couch."

sentences.zh: 于是沛公輟洗，起衣，延食其上坐，謝之。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Lord of Pei stopped the foot-washing, rose, straightened his clothes, led Li Shiqi to the seat of honor, and apologized.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei broke off the washing, stood, adjusted his robe, seated Li Shiqi in the place of honor, and apologized.

sentences.zh: 食其因言六國從衡時，沛公喜，賜食其食，問曰：「計安出？」

translations.literal: Li Shiqi thereupon spoke of the vertical and horizontal alliances of the Six States; the Lord of Pei was pleased, gave Li Shiqi a meal, and asked, "What plan should we adopt?"
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi then discoursed on the Warring States leagues of north–south and east–west. The Lord of Pei was delighted, fed him, and asked, "What is your counsel?"

sentences.zh: 食其曰：「足下起瓦合之卒，收散亂之兵，不滿万人，欲以徑人強秦，此所謂探虎口者也。

translations.literal: Li Shiqi said, "You, sir, have raised troops like tiles hastily mortared and gathered scattered soldiers numbering less than ten thousand men; yet you wish to drive straight into mighty Qin—this is what people call sticking your hand into a tiger's mouth.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi said, "You have patched together green troops and scraped up stragglers—fewer than ten thousand—and you propose to plunge straight at powerful Qin. That is what they call thrusting your fist into a tiger's jaws.

sentences.zh: 夫陳留，天下之沖，四通五達之郊也，今其城中又多積粟，臣知其令，今請使，令下足下。

translations.literal: Chenliu is the crossroads of the empire, a hub where four ways meet and five roads join; now its walls hold great stores of grain. I am acquainted with its magistrate; allow me to go as envoy and make him submit to you.
translations.idiomatic: Chenliu sits at the heart of the realm, where every road runs; the city is full of grain. I know the magistrate—send me, and I will bring him over to your side.

sentences.zh: 即不听，足下舉兵攻之，臣為內應。」

translations.literal: If he will not listen, you raise your army to attack him, and I shall act as your agent within."
translations.idiomatic: If he refuses, you strike the walls, and I will open the gates from within."

sentences.zh: 于是遣食其往，沛公引兵隨之，遂下陳留。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent Li Shiqi ahead, and the Lord of Pei led his troops after him; they thereupon took Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: He dispatched Li Shiqi, followed with the army, and Chenliu fell.

sentences.zh: 號食其為廣野君。

translations.literal: He styled Li Shiqi Lord of the Vast Wilds.
translations.idiomatic: He ennobled Li Shiqi as Lord of the Vast Wilds.

sentences.zh: 食其言弟商，使將數千人從沛公西南略地。

translations.literal: Li Shiqi spoke of his younger brother Shang, and had him lead several thousand men to seize territory to the southwest for the Lord of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi recommended his brother Shang, who led several thousand men to campaign southwest for the Lord of Pei.

sentences.zh: 食其常為說客，馳使諸侯。

translations.literal: Li Shiqi often served as persuader, galloping on missions to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi served habitually as envoy, riding at speed to the feudal courts.

sentences.zh: 漢三年秋，項羽擊漢，拔滎陽，漢兵遁保鞏。

translations.literal: In the autumn of the third year of Han, Xiang Yu struck Han, took Yingyang, and the Han troops withdrew to hold Gong.
translations.idiomatic: In the autumn of Han year 3, Xiang Yu attacked Han, captured Yingyang, and Han forces fell back to defend Gong.

sentences.zh: 楚人聞韓信破趙，彭越數反梁地，則分兵救之。

translations.literal: The people of Chu, hearing that Han Xin had destroyed Zhao and that Peng Yue had repeatedly rebelled in the lands of Liang, then divided their troops to rescue those places.
translations.idiomatic: When Chu learned that Han Xin had crushed Zhao and that Peng Yue kept rising in Liang, they peeled off forces to deal with each threat.

sentences.zh: 韓信方東擊齊，漢王數困滎陽、成皋，計欲捐成皋以東，屯鞏、雒以距楚。

translations.literal: Han Xin was at that moment striking east against Qi; the King of Han was repeatedly trapped at Yingyang and Chenggao and planned to abandon the territory east of Chenggao and station troops at Gong and Luo to block Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin was turning east against Qi. The King of Han had been pinned again and again at Yingyang and Chenggao and thought of yielding everything east of Chenggao and holding the line at Gong and Luoyang against Chu.

sentences.zh: 食其因曰：「臣聞之，知天之天者，王事可成；

translations.literal: Li Shiqi therefore said, "I have heard it said: he who knows Heaven's Heaven may accomplish the kingly enterprise;
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi said, "I have heard: only he who grasps what Heaven means by 'Heaven' can finish the work of kingship;

sentences.zh: 不知天之天者，王事不可成。

translations.literal: he who does not know Heaven's Heaven cannot accomplish the kingly enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: he who misses that meaning will never bring it off.

sentences.zh: 王者以民為天，而民以食為天。

translations.literal: The true king takes the people as his Heaven, and the people take food as their Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: For a true king, the people are Heaven; for the people, grain is Heaven.

sentences.zh: 夫敖倉，天下轉輸久矣，臣聞其下乃有臧粟甚多。

translations.literal: As for the Ao Granary, the empire's transport of grain has long converged there; I have heard that beneath it lie stored grains in very great quantity.
translations.idiomatic: The Ao Granary has fed the realm for ages; I am told vast reserves lie beneath it.

sentences.zh: 楚人拔滎陽，不堅守敖倉，乃引而東，令適卒分守成皋，此乃天所以資漢。

translations.literal: The men of Chu took Yingyang but did not stubbornly hold the Ao Granary; instead they drew eastward and had convict soldiers hold Chenggao in detachments—this is Heaven lending its aid to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Chu seized Yingyang yet failed to lock down the Ao Granary; they pulled east and left penal troops to garrison Chenggao in pieces—Heaven is handing Han an opening.

sentences.zh: 方今楚易取而漢后卻，自奪便，臣竊以為過矣。

translations.literal: At present Chu would be easy to take while Han hangs back; to throw away your own advantage—privately I think it a mistake.
translations.idiomatic: Now Chu is there for the taking while Han shrinks back; to surrender the initiative yourself—I call that a blunder.

sentences.zh: 且兩雄不俱立，楚、漢久相持不決，百姓騷動，海內搖蕩，農夫釋耒，紅女下机，天下之心未有所定也。

translations.literal: Moreover two champions cannot long stand together; Chu and Han have faced each other endlessly, the hundred surnames are in turmoil, the realm shakes, farmers drop their plows and weaving girls leave their looms—the hearts of all under Heaven have not yet settled on whom to follow.
translations.idiomatic: Two titans cannot share one throne. Chu and Han have stalemated so long that the common people reel, the realm quakes, plows rust in the fields and shuttles lie idle—nowhere has the world fixed its loyalty.

sentences.zh: 愿足下急复進兵，收取滎陽，据敖庚之粟，塞成皋之險，杜太行之道，距飛狐之口，守白馬之津，以示諸侯形制之勢，則天下知所歸矣。

translations.literal: I beg you, sir, to press forward at once, recover Yingyang, seize the grain at the Ao Granary (敖庚), block the narrows of Chenggao, choke the Taihang road, guard the mouth at Feihu, hold the ford at Baima, and thereby show the feudal lords the shape of power—then all under Heaven will know where to turn.
translations.idiomatic: I urge you: advance now, retake Yingyang, command the grain at Ao, seal the Chenggao defiles, plug the Taihang passes, bar Feihu, secure the Baima crossing—show the lords the map of power, and the empire will know its master.

sentences.zh: 方今燕、趙已定，唯齊未下。

translations.literal: At present Yan and Zhao are already settled; only Qi has not yet submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Yan and Zhao are pacified; only Qi still holds out.

sentences.zh: 今田廣据千里之齊，田間將二十万之眾軍于歷城，諸田宗強，負海岱，阻河濟，南近楚，齊人多變詐，足下雖遣數十万師，未可以歲月破也。

translations.literal: Now Tian Guang holds Qi of a thousand li, Tian Jian leads two hundred thousand troops encamped at Licheng, the Tian clan is strong, backed by sea and Mount Dai, blocked by the Yellow River and the Ji, close to Chu to the south, and the men of Qi are full of shifts and stratagems—even if you, sir, sent several hundred thousand troops, you could not break them in a year or a season.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Guang rules a thousand li of Qi; Tian Jian camps two hundred thousand at Licheng; the Tian lineage is entrenched between sea and Tai, river and Ji, with Chu at their flank; Qi men are wily. Hundreds of thousands would not crack them in a season.

sentences.zh: 臣請得奉明詔說齊王使為漢而稱東籓。」

translations.literal: I beg leave to carry your clear decree and persuade the King of Qi to become Han's vassal in the east."
translations.idiomatic: Give me your mandate: I will talk the King of Qi into declaring himself Han's eastern bulwark."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign said, "Well said."

sentences.zh: 乃從其畫，复守敖倉，而使食其說齊王，曰：「王知天下之所歸乎？」

translations.literal: He followed this plan, again held the Ao Granary, and sent Li Shiqi to persuade the King of Qi, saying, "Does Your Majesty know where the empire is turning?"
translations.idiomatic: He adopted the plan, secured the Ao Granary again, and sent Li Shiqi to the King of Qi. "Do you know," he asked, "where the world's allegiance is going?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「不知也。」

translations.literal: The king said, "I do not know."
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "I do not."

sentences.zh: 曰：「知天下之所歸，則齊國可得而有也；

translations.literal: He said, "If you know where the empire is turning, then you may possess the state of Qi;
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi said, "Know that, and you may keep Qi;

sentences.zh: 若不知天下之所歸，即齊國未可保也。」

translations.literal: if you do not know where the empire is turning, then you cannot even preserve Qi."
translations.idiomatic: miss it, and not even Qi will be safe."

sentences.zh: 齊王曰：「天下何歸？」

translations.literal: The King of Qi said, "Where does all under Heaven turn?"
translations.idiomatic: The King of Qi asked, "Where does the realm lean?"

sentences.zh: 食其曰：「天下歸漢。」

translations.literal: Li Shiqi said, "All under Heaven turns to Han."
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi answered, "Toward Han."

sentences.zh: 齊王曰：「先生何以言之？」

translations.literal: The King of Qi said, "On what grounds does the master say so?"
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "What makes you say that?"

sentences.zh: 曰：「漢王与項王戮力西面擊秦，約先入咸陽者王之，項王背約不与，而王之漢中。

translations.literal: He said, "The King of Han and King Xiang joined strength to strike Qin from the west, agreeing that whoever first entered Xianyang would be king there; King Xiang broke the covenant and did not grant it, but instead made him king in Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He said, "The King of Han and Xiang Yu struck Qin together from the west and swore that whoever reached Xianyang first would rule there. Xiang Yu broke the pact and gave Hanzhong instead.

sentences.zh: 項王遷殺義帝，漢王起蜀漢之兵擊三秦，出關而責義帝之負處，收天下之兵，立諸侯之后。

translations.literal: King Xiang removed and killed the Righteous Emperor; the King of Han raised troops from Ba and Shu, struck the Three Qin, came out of the passes to reproach the place where the Righteous Emperor was wronged, gathered the arms of the empire, and set up the descendants of the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu banished and murdered the Righteous Emperor. The King of Han marched from Ba and Shu, overran the Three Qin, emerged from the passes denouncing that crime, rallied the hosts of the realm, and restored the bloodlines of the old ruling houses.

sentences.zh: 降城即以侯其將，得賂則以分其士，与天下同其利，豪英賢材皆樂為之用。

translations.literal: When a city submitted he at once ennobled its general as marquis; when he obtained spoils he shared them with his soldiers—he shares profit with all under Heaven, and heroes and worthy talent are all glad to serve him.
translations.idiomatic: Cities that yielded won their generals marquisates; booty went to the ranks. He shares gain with the world, so every able man rallies to him.

sentences.zh: 諸侯之兵四面而至，蜀漢之粟方船而下。

translations.literal: The troops of the feudal lords arrive from the four quarters, and the grain of Ba and Shu comes downriver in linked barges.
translations.idiomatic: Allied armies converge from every side; grain barges from Ba and Shu stream down the rivers.

sentences.zh: 項王有背約之名，殺義帝之負；

translations.literal: King Xiang has the reputation of breaking the covenant and bears the guilt of killing the Righteous Emperor;
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu wears the name of oath-breaker and the blood of the Righteous Emperor;

sentences.zh: 于人之功無所記，于人之罪無所忘；

translations.literal: for others' merits he keeps no record, for others' faults he never forgets;
translations.idiomatic: he remembers no one's deeds and forgets no one's faults;

sentences.zh: 戰胜而不得其賞，拔城而不得其封；

translations.literal: those who win in battle receive no reward, those who take cities receive no enfeoffment;
translations.idiomatic: victors go unrewarded, conquerors unenfeoffed;

sentences.zh: 非項氏莫得用事；

translations.literal: none but those surnamed Xiang may hold office;
translations.idiomatic: only Xiang clansmen hold command;

sentences.zh: 為人刻印，玩而不能授；

translations.literal: when he has seals carved for others, he toys with them and cannot bestow them;
translations.idiomatic: he grinds out seals yet cannot bring himself to hand them over;

sentences.zh: 攻城得賂，積財而不能賞。

translations.literal: when he takes spoils in a siege, he piles up wealth and cannot give rewards.
translations.idiomatic: siege loot piles in his treasury while his captains go empty.

sentences.zh: 天下畔之，賢材怨之，而莫為之用。

translations.literal: All under Heaven rebel against him; worthy talent resents him, and none will serve him.
translations.idiomatic: The world has turned; able men loathe him and will not fight for him.

sentences.zh: 故天下之士歸于漢王，可坐而策也。

translations.literal: Therefore the knights of the empire are going to the King of Han; you may sit and reckon it out.
translations.idiomatic: That is why every strategist is drifting to the King of Han—you can tally the rest from your chair.

sentences.zh: 夫漢王發蜀漢，定三秦；

translations.literal: The King of Han set out from Ba and Shu and settled the Three Qin;
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han left Ba–Shu and locked down the Three Qin;

sentences.zh: 涉西河之外，授上党之兵；

translations.literal: he crossed beyond the west bank of the Yellow River and handed over the troops of Shangdang;
translations.idiomatic: he crossed the west river, fed Shangdang's armies into his host;

sentences.zh: 下井陘，誅成安君；

translations.literal: he descended the Jingxing road and executed Lord Cheng'an;
translations.idiomatic: he came down Jingxing and executed Lord Cheng'an;

sentences.zh: 破北魏，舉三十二城：此黃帝之兵，非人之力，天之福今。

translations.literal: he broke Northern Wei and took thirty-two cities: these are the Yellow Emperor's host, not the strength of men—the blessing of Heaven is here now.
translations.idiomatic: he shattered Northern Wei and took thirty-two cities—this is an army Heaven sends, not human effort alone; the mandate is manifest now.

sentences.zh: 今已据敖倉之粟，塞成皋之險，守白馬之津，杜太行之厄，距飛狐之口，天下后服者先亡矣。

translations.literal: He has now seized the grain of the Ao Granary, blocked the narrows of Chenggao, guarded the ford at Baima, plugged the crisis point of Taihang, and barred the mouth at Feihu; whoever submits late among the empire will perish first.
translations.idiomatic: He already holds Ao's granaries, seals Chenggao, guards Baima, chokes Taihang, and bars Feihu—whoever comes last to kneel will be first to fall.

sentences.zh: 王疾下漢王，齊國社稷可得而保也；

translations.literal: If Your Majesty quickly submits to the King of Han, you may preserve the altars of soil and grain of Qi;
translations.idiomatic: Submit promptly to the King of Han, and you may save Qi's altars;

sentences.zh: 不下漢王，危亡可立而待也。」

translations.literal: if you do not submit to the King of Han, danger and destruction may stand and wait."
translations.idiomatic: delay, and ruin will be on you before you turn around."

sentences.zh: 田廣以為然，乃听食其，罷歷下兵守戰備，与食其日縱酒。

translations.literal: Tian Guang thought it right, followed Li Shiqi, dismissed the troops defending Licheng from combat readiness, and daily drank deep with Li Shiqi.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Guang agreed, took Li Shiqi's advice, stood down the Licheng garrison, and feasted with him day after day.

sentences.zh: 韓信聞食其馮軾下齊七十餘城，乃夜度兵平原襲齊。

translations.literal: When Han Xin heard that Li Shiqi, riding in his carriage, had brought down more than seventy cities of Qi, he by night moved troops across Pingyuan and struck Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin learned that Li Shiqi had taken seventy-odd Qi cities from his chariot seat and slipped an army across Pingyuan by night to hit Qi.

sentences.zh: 齊王田廣聞漢兵至，以為食其賣己，乃亨食其，引兵走。

translations.literal: When King Tian Guang of Qi heard that Han troops had arrived, he thought Li Shiqi had betrayed him; he therefore had Li Shiqi boiled alive and led his troops in flight.
translations.idiomatic: When Han troops appeared, King Tian Guang decided Li Shiqi had sold him out—he had Li Shiqi boiled alive and fled with his army.

sentences.zh: 漢十二年，曲周侯酈商以丞相將兵擊黥布，有功。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year of Han, Marquis of Quzhou Li Shang led troops as chancellor against Qing Bu and achieved merit.
translations.idiomatic: In Han year 12, Quzhou marquis Li Shang commanded an army as chancellor against Ying Bu and distinguished himself.

sentences.zh: 高祖舉功臣，思食其。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor raised the merits of his ministers and remembered Li Shiqi.
translations.idiomatic: When the High Ancestor ranked his meritorious officials, his thoughts returned to Li Shiqi.

sentences.zh: 食其子疥數將兵，上以其父故，封疥為高梁侯。

translations.literal: Li Shiqi's son Jie had repeatedly led troops; because of his father the sovereign enfeoffed Jie as marquis of Gaoliang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Jie had often held command; out of regard for the father, the emperor enfeoffed Jie as marquis of Gaoliang.

sentences.zh: 后更食武陽，卒，子遂嗣。

translations.literal: Later his fief was changed to Wuyang; when he died, his son Sui inherited.
translations.idiomatic: The fief was later moved to Wuyang; on his death his son Sui succeeded.

sentences.zh: 三世，侯平有罪，國除。

translations.literal: After three generations Marquis Ping committed a crime and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Three generations on, Marquis Ping was convicted and the marquisate was struck off.

sentences.zh: 陸賈，楚人也。

translations.literal: Lu Jia was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia was a native of Chu.

sentences.zh: 以客從高祖定天下，名有口辯，居左右，常使諸侯。

translations.literal: As a retainer he followed the High Ancestor to settle the empire; he was famed for eloquence, attended at his left and right, and was often sent on missions to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: He entered the High Ancestor's service as a client adviser, helped pacify the realm, and was celebrated for his tongue—always at court, constantly dispatched to the feudal courts.

sentences.zh: 時中國初定，尉佗平南越，因王之。

translations.literal: At that time the central lands had just been pacified; Wei Tuo had pacified the Southern Yue and thereupon made himself king there.
translations.idiomatic: China had barely been pacified when Wei Tuo conquered the Southern Yue and crowned himself its king.

sentences.zh: 高祖使賈賜佗印為南越王。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor sent Lu Jia to present Tuo with a seal as King of Southern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor sent Lu Jia to invest him with the seal of King of Southern Yue.

sentences.zh: 賈至，尉佗魋結箕踞見賈。

translations.literal: When Lu Jia arrived, Wei Tuo met him with a Chu-style topknot and sitting with legs sprawled like a basket.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia found Wei Tuo in a Chu-style chignon, legs folded arrogantly in the Yue manner.

sentences.zh: 賈因說佗曰：「足下中國人，親戚昆弟墳墓在真定。

translations.literal: Lu Jia therefore said to Tuo, "You, sir, are a man of the central lands; your kin, brothers, and ancestral graves are at Zhending.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia said, "You are a man of the heartland; kin and ancestors lie at Zhending.

sentences.zh: 今足下反天性，棄冠帶，欲以區區之越与天子抗衡為敵國，禍且及身矣。

translations.literal: Now you, sir, turn against your inborn nature, cast off cap and sash, and wish with the narrow territory of Yue to stand against the Son of Heaven as a rival state—disaster will soon reach your person.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you repudiate your breeding, throw off Chinese dress, and think this pocket kingdom can defy the Son of Heaven—ruin will find you soon.

sentences.zh: 夫秦失其正，諸侯豪桀并起，唯漢王先入關，据咸陽。

translations.literal: When Qin lost the right way, the feudal lords and heroic champions rose together; only the King of Han was first to enter the passes and occupy Xianyang.
translations.idiomatic: Qin lost the Mandate and heroes rose everywhere; only the King of Han entered the passes first and took Xianyang.

sentences.zh: 項籍背約，自立為西楚霸王，諸侯皆屬，可謂至強矣。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu broke the covenant, set himself up as Hegemon-King of Western Chu, and the feudal lords all submitted—he may be called supremely strong.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu tore up the agreement, styled himself Hegemon-King of Western Chu, and brought the lords to heel—none seemed mightier.

sentences.zh: 然漢王起巴、蜀，鞭笞天下，劫諸侯，遂誅項羽。

translations.literal: Yet the King of Han rose from Ba and Shu, whipped the empire, coerced the feudal lords, and thereupon executed Xiang Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Still the King of Han rose from Ba and Shu, drove the realm before him, bent the lords to his will, and cut down Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 五年之間，海內平定，此非人力，天之所建也。

translations.literal: Within five years the inner seas were pacified—this is not human strength; it is what Heaven has established.
translations.idiomatic: Inside five years the realm was still—no mere human deed, but Heaven's founding work.

sentences.zh: 天也聞君王王南越，而不助天下誅暴逆，將相欲移兵而誅王，天子怜百姓新勞苦，且休之，遣臣授君王印，剖符通使。

translations.literal: Heaven has heard that you, king, have made yourself king in Southern Yue yet do not aid all under Heaven in punishing the violent and rebellious; the generals and ministers wish to shift troops to punish you, but the Son of Heaven pities the hundred surnames who are newly worn with toil and for the moment rests them, sending me to present you, king, with a seal, to split tallies and open communication.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven sees you ruling Yue yet doing nothing to help crush the rebels; your own ministers would march against you, but the Son of Heaven pities a weary people and stays their hand—he sends me only to hand you the seal and open tally traffic.

sentences.zh: 君王宜郊迎，北面稱臣，乃欲以新造未集之越屈強于此。

translations.literal: You, king, ought to go out to the suburbs to welcome the envoy, face north and declare yourself subject; yet you wish to use a newly made, still unsettled Yue to show obstinacy here.
translations.idiomatic: You should meet me beyond the walls, turn north, and own yourself vassal—instead you flex a half-built Yue as if it were a power.

sentences.zh: 漢誠聞之，掘燒君王先人冢墓，夷种宗族，使一偏將將十万眾臨越，即越殺王降漢，如反覆手耳。」

translations.literal: If Han truly hears of this, they will dig up and burn the tombs of your, king's, forebears, exterminate your clan by kindred, and send a single deputy general with a hundred thousand troops to the border of Yue—then even if Yue killed its king and surrendered to Han, it would be like turning the hand over."
translations.idiomatic: If the court learns of this insult, they will raze your ancestors' graves and extirpate your lineage, then send one general and a hundred thousand men to the frontier—Yue could kill you tomorrow and switch sides as easily as flipping a palm."

sentences.zh: 于是佗乃蹶然起坐，謝賈曰：「居蠻夷中久，殊失禮義。」

translations.literal: Thereupon Tuo suddenly straightened up from sitting, apologized to Lu Jia, saying, "I have dwelt long among the Man and Yi and have utterly lost ritual and rightness."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Tuo jolted upright, apologized, and said, "Too long among barbarians—I have forgotten courtesy."

sentences.zh: 因問賈曰：「我孰与蕭何、曹參、韓信賢？」

translations.literal: He then asked Lu Jia, "Compared to Xiao He, Cao Can, and Han Xin, which am I the worthier?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked, "Am I the better man, or Xiao He, Cao Can, and Han Xin?"

sentences.zh: 賈曰：「王似賢也。」

translations.literal: Lu Jia said, "Your Majesty seems the worthier."
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia said, "You seem their superior."

sentences.zh: 复問曰：「我孰与皇帝賢？」

translations.literal: He asked again, "Compared to the Emperor, which am I the worthier?"
translations.idiomatic: He pressed, "And compared to the Emperor?"

sentences.zh: 賈曰「皇帝起丰沛，討暴秦，誅強楚，為天下興利除害，繼五帝三王之業，統天下，理中國。

translations.literal: Lu Jia said, "The Emperor rose from Feng and Pei, punished violent Qin, destroyed mighty Chu, brought benefit to all under Heaven and removed harm for them, continued the enterprises of the Five Emperors and Three Kings, unified the empire, and ordered the central lands.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia answered, "The Emperor rose from Feng and Pei, crushed Qin, broke Chu, worked good and removed evil for the world, carried on the legacy of the Five Emperors and Three Kings, united the realm, and governed China.

sentences.zh: 中國之人以億計，地方万里，居天下之膏腴，人眾車輿，万物殷富，政由一家，自天地剖判未始有也。

translations.literal: The people of the central lands number in the hundreds of millions; the territory is ten thousand li; they occupy the fat lands of all under Heaven; people and carts are many, the myriad things are abundant, government proceeds from one house—since Heaven and Earth were first divided apart, there has never been such a thing.
translations.idiomatic: China counts its people in the hundreds of millions; its soil runs ten thousand li of the empire's richest ground—crowds, carts, overflowing wealth, rule from a single throne—nothing like it has existed since creation.

sentences.zh: 今王眾不過數万，皆蠻夷，崎嶇山海間，譬如漢一郡，王何乃比于漢！」

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty's host numbers no more than several tens of thousands, and all are Man and Yi, in the ruggedness between mountains and sea; it is like a single commandery of Han—how can Your Majesty compare yourself to Han!"
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty commands a few tens of thousands of frontier tribesmen in rough hills by the sea—one Han commandery, at best. How can you measure yourself against the Han empire!"

sentences.zh: 佗大笑曰：「吾不起中國，故王此。

translations.literal: Tuo laughed greatly and said, "I did not rise up in the central lands; therefore I have made myself king here."
translations.idiomatic: "I never built a career in China—that is why I rule in this corner of the world."

sentences.zh: 使我居中國，何遽不若漢？」

translations.literal: If you had placed me in the central lands, how could I quickly fail to match Han?"
translations.idiomatic: "Had Heaven planted me in the heartland, I would hardly have yielded to Han."

sentences.zh: 乃大說賈，留与飲數月。

translations.literal: Thereupon he took great delight in Jia, detained him, and drank with him for several months.
translations.idiomatic: He warmed to Lu Jia at once and kept him for months of feasting.

sentences.zh: 曰：「越中無足与語，至生來，令我日聞所不聞。」

translations.literal: He said, "In Yue there is no one worth conversing with; since you, sir, arrived, you have caused me daily to hear what I had never heard."
translations.idiomatic: "Yue offered no one I could talk to; since you came I learn something new every day."

sentences.zh: 賜賈橐中裝直千金，它送亦千金。

translations.literal: He presented Jia with travel gear in his bags worth a thousand in gold; other parting gifts also came to a thousand in gold.
translations.idiomatic: He gave Lu Jia a thousand in gold of traveling finery and another thousand in farewell gifts.

sentences.zh: 賈卒拜佗為南越王，令稱臣奉漢約。

translations.literal: Jia finally invested Tuo as King of Southern Yue, ordering him to declare himself subject and uphold Han's agreements.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia completed the investiture, bound him to acknowledge Han as suzerain, and secured his adherence to the treaty.

sentences.zh: 歸報，高帝大說，拜賈為太中大夫。

translations.literal: When he returned to report, the High Ancestor was greatly pleased and appointed Jia Grand Palace Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu was delighted with the report and named him Grandee of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 賈時時前說稱《詩》、《書》。

translations.literal: Jia from time to time stepped forward to discourse and cite the *Odes* and the *Documents*.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia kept pressing the classics—the *Book of Odes* and the *Book of Documents*—upon the throne.

sentences.zh: 高帝罵之曰：「乃公居馬上得之，安事《詩》、《書》！」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor reviled him, saying, "Your old man won it on horseback—what business have the *Odes* and the *Documents*!"
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu snapped, "Your old man seized the realm from the saddle—what use has he for poetry and history?"

sentences.zh: 賈曰：「馬上得之，宁可以馬上治乎？

translations.literal: Jia said, "You may obtain it on horseback, but can you govern it on horseback?
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia answered, "You may conquer from horseback, but you cannot administer from the saddle.

sentences.zh: 且湯、武逆取而以順守之，文帝并用，長久之術也。

translations.literal: Moreover Tang and Wu took power against the current yet held it by ruling in accord with legitimacy; when civil and martial policies are both employed, that is the method of long endurance."
translations.idiomatic: Tang and Wu wrested the mandate by force yet kept it with righteous rule; the lasting throne pairs civil arts with military awe."

sentences.zh: 昔者吳王夫差、智伯极武而亡；

translations.literal: In former times King Fuchai of Wu and the Earl of Zhi were extreme in martialism and perished;
translations.idiomatic: King Fuchai of Wu and the Earl of Zhi rode military force to the limit—and were destroyed.

sentences.zh: 秦任刑法不變，卒滅趙氏。

translations.literal: Qin relied on punishments and laws without changing; in the end it extinguished its own ruling house.
translations.idiomatic: Qin trusted harsh statutes and never reformed, until its own dynasty burned out.

sentences.zh: 鄉使秦以并天下，行仁義，法先圣，陛下安得而有之？」

translations.literal: If formerly Qin, having already annexed all under Heaven, had practiced benevolence and right and modeled the former sages, how would Your Majesty have been able to possess it?"
translations.idiomatic: "Had Qin, once it united the realm, turned to benevolence and taken the ancient sages as its mirror, how could you, sire, ever have worn the crown?"

sentences.zh: 高帝不懌，有慚色，謂賈曰：「試為我著秦所以失天下，吾所以得之者，及古成敗之國。」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor was displeased, showed a shamefaced look, and said to Jia, "Try to write for me Qin's reasons for losing the empire, my reasons for gaining it, and the successes and failures of states in antiquity."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor looked sour, then sheepish, and told Lu Jia, "Draft for me why Qin lost the empire, why I won it, and how kingdoms of old rose or fell."

sentences.zh: 賈凡著十二篇。

translations.literal: Jia in all composed twelve chapters.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia produced twelve essays.

sentences.zh: 每奏一篇，高帝未嘗不稱善，左右呼万歲，稱其書曰《新語》。

translations.literal: Each time he presented a chapter, the High Ancestor never failed to praise it; those at his left and right shouted "Long life!" and they styled his book *New Sayings*.
translations.idiomatic: Every chapter he read aloud drew Gaozu's praise; courtiers cheered "Wan sui!" and the collection was titled *New Discourses*.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，呂太后用事，欲王諸呂，畏大臣及有口者。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Hui, Empress Dowager Lü wielded power, wished to make the Lüs kings, and feared the great ministers and men with eloquent tongues.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui, Empress Dowager Lü held the government, planned to ennoble her clan as kings, and dreaded senior ministers and sharp debaters.

sentences.zh: 賈自度不能爭之，乃病免。

translations.literal: Jia estimated that he could not contend with this; he thereupon resigned on grounds of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia knew he could not outmaneuver her, pleaded illness, and left office.

sentences.zh: 以好疇田地善，往家焉。

translations.literal: Because the fields and lands of Haozhi were good, he went and made his home there.
translations.idiomatic: He retired to Haozhi for its rich soil and settled his household.

sentences.zh: 有五男，乃出所使越橐中裝，賣千金，分其子，子二百金，令為生產。

translations.literal: He had five sons; he then took out the baggage-trappings from his mission to Yue, sold them for a thousand in gold, divided it among his sons—two hundred to each son—and ordered them to pursue productive livelihoods.
translations.idiomatic: He had five sons. He cashed out the treasure he had carried back from Yue—worth a thousand in gold—and gave each son two hundred, telling them to invest it in their futures.

sentences.zh: 賈常乘安車駟馬，從歌鼓瑟侍者十人，寶劍直百金，謂其子曰：「与女約：過女，女給人馬酒食极欲，十日而更。

translations.literal: Jia often rode in a cushioned carriage with four horses, with ten attendants who sang and played the se; he had a precious sword worth a hundred in gold. He said to his sons, "I make a covenant with you: when I pass your house, you shall supply men, horses, wine, and food to the utmost of my desire, for ten days, then I shall move on.
translations.idiomatic: He traveled in a four-horse covered carriage with ten musicians in train and a sword worth a hundred in gold. "Here is our bargain," he told his sons: "Whenever I visit, you will mount me, pour the wine, and lay the table to my heart's content for ten days—then I go on."

sentences.zh: 所死家，得寶劍車騎侍從者。

translations.literal: In whichever house I die, that son shall obtain the precious sword, the chariot, the horses, and the attendants.
translations.idiomatic: "Whichever roof shelters my death keeps the sword, the carriage, the horses, and the retinue."

sentences.zh: 一歲中以往來過它客，率不過再過，數擊鮮，毋久溷女為也。」

translations.literal: Within a year, in coming and going past other houses as a guest, I shall generally not visit more than twice; I shall often bring fresh-killed game—do not let me long trouble you in this."
translations.idiomatic: "In any given year I will rarely call on any of you more than twice, and I will bring fresh game often—so I will not wear out my welcome."

sentences.zh: 呂太后時，王諸呂，諸呂擅權，欲劫少主，危劉氏。

translations.literal: In Empress Dowager Lü's time the Lüs were made kings; the Lüs monopolized power, wished to coerce the young sovereign, and endangered the house of Liu.
translations.idiomatic: When the dowager ennobled the Lüs they seized the levers of power, plotted to hold the boy emperor hostage, and menaced the Liu lineage.

sentences.zh: 右丞相陳平患之，力不能爭，恐禍及己。

translations.literal: Right Chancellor Chen Ping worried over it; his strength could not contend, and he feared disaster would reach himself.
translations.idiomatic: Right Chancellor Chen Ping was sick with worry—too weak to resist, yet afraid the blow would fall on him.

sentences.zh: 平常燕居深念。

translations.literal: Ping often, dwelling at leisure, pondered deeply.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping often sat alone at home, brooding.

sentences.zh: 賈往，不請，直入坐，陳平方念，不見賈。

translations.literal: When Jia went, without requesting announcement he straightway entered and sat down; Chen Ping was just then pondering and did not notice Jia.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia walked in unbidden, took a seat, while Chen Ping remained lost in thought and never saw him enter.

sentences.zh: 賈曰：「何念深也？」

translations.literal: Jia said, "What are you pondering so deeply?"
translations.idiomatic: "What has you sunk so deep in thought?"

sentences.zh: 平曰：「生揣我何念？」

translations.literal: Ping said, "Sir, what do you surmise I am pondering?"
translations.idiomatic: "Can you guess what troubles me?"

sentences.zh: 賈曰：「足下位為上相，食三萬戶侯，可謂極富貴無欲矣。

translations.literal: Jia said, "Your foot stands as chief minister and draws the income of a thirty-thousand-household marquis; one may call this the utmost of wealth and honor, without further desire.
translations.idiomatic: "You are chief minister and enjoy a thirty-thousand-household stipend—you have climbed to wealth and rank and should want for nothing.

sentences.zh: 然有憂念，不過患諸呂、少主耳。」

translations.literal: Yet there is anxious thought—nothing beyond worrying about the various Lüs and the young lord."
translations.idiomatic: Yet you are anxious—and it can only be the Lü cabal and the child emperor."

sentences.zh: 陳平曰：「然。

translations.literal: Chen Ping said, "It is so.
translations.idiomatic: "True."

sentences.zh: 為之奈何？」

translations.literal: What is to be done about it?"
translations.idiomatic: "What can be done?"

sentences.zh: 賈曰：「天下安，注意相；

translations.literal: Jia said, "When all under Heaven is at peace, attention fixes on the chancellor;
translations.idiomatic: "When the realm is calm, eyes turn to the chancellor;

sentences.zh: 天下危，注意將。

translations.literal: when all under Heaven is in peril, attention fixes on the general.
translations.idiomatic: when it totters, eyes turn to the general.

sentences.zh: 將相和，則士豫附；

translations.literal: When general and chancellor are in harmony, then knights gladly attach themselves;
translations.idiomatic: If those two stand together, bold men will flock to you;

sentences.zh: 士豫附，天下雖有變，則權不分。

translations.literal: when knights gladly attach themselves, though all under Heaven has changes, power is not divided.
translations.idiomatic: with talent at your back, even upheaval will not split your authority.

sentences.zh: 權不分，為社稷計，在兩君掌握耳。

translations.literal: When authority is not split, the calculation for the altars of soil and grain lies in the palms of you two alone.
translations.idiomatic: So long as you keep command undivided, the fate of the altars rests in the hands of you two alone.

sentences.zh: 臣常欲謂太尉絳侯，絳侯與我戲，易吾言。

translations.literal: Your subject has constantly wished to speak to Grand Commandant Marquis of Jiang, but Marquis of Jiang jests with me and treats my words lightly.
translations.idiomatic: "I have long wanted to counsel Grand Commandant Zhou Bo, but he jokes with me and brushes off what I say."

sentences.zh: 君何不交驩太尉，深相結？」

translations.literal: Why do you, sir, not exchange cordiality with the Grand Commandant and tie your bond deeply?"
translations.idiomatic: "Win his trust openly and bind him to you—that is the path."

sentences.zh: 為陳平畫呂氏數事。

translations.literal: He laid out for Chen Ping several stratagems concerning the Lü clan.
translations.idiomatic: He sketched a series of moves against the Lüs for Chen Ping.

sentences.zh: 平用其計，乃以五百金為絳侯壽，厚縣樂飲太尉，太尉亦報如之。

translations.literal: Ping used his plan; he then with five hundred in gold celebrated Marquis of Jiang's birthday, lavishly provided music and drink for the Grand Commandant, and the Grand Commandant reciprocated in like manner.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping followed the advice, sent five hundred in gold for Zhou Bo's birthday, and feasted him royally; the grand commandant returned the courtesy in kind.

sentences.zh: 兩人深相結，呂氏謀益壞。

translations.literal: The two men bound themselves deeply; the Lü clan's plots grew ever more broken.
translations.idiomatic: The two became confidants, and the Lü conspiracy began to fray.

sentences.zh: 陳平乃以奴婢百人，車馬五十乘，錢五百萬，遺賈為食飲費。

translations.literal: Chen Ping then with a hundred slave girls, fifty teams of chariots and horses, and five million cash gifted Jia for the expenses of feasting and drinking.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping gave him a hundred maids, fifty carriages, and five million cash to bankroll his entertaining.

sentences.zh: 賈以此遊漢廷公卿間，名聲籍甚。

translations.literal: Jia by this means moved among the dukes and ministers of the Han court; his name and reputation became very great.
translations.idiomatic: With that stake Lu Jia circulated among Han's grandees and grew famous overnight.

sentences.zh: 及誅呂氏，立孝文，賈頗有力。

translations.literal: When the Lü clan was executed and Emperor Xiaowen was established, Jia had considerable force in the matter.
translations.idiomatic: When the Lüs fell and Emperor Wen ascended, Lu Jia had done heavy lifting behind the scenes.

sentences.zh: 孝文即位，欲使人之南越，丞相平乃言賈為太中大夫，往使尉佗，去黃屋稱制，令比諸侯，皆如意指。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen assumed the throne he wished to send someone to Southern Yue; Chancellor Ping thereupon recommended Jia as Grand Palace Grandee to go on mission to Wei Tuo, remove the yellow canopy and imperial forms of address, and order him to rank with the feudal lords—all accorded with the sovereign's intent.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen meant to reopen ties with Yue; Chancellor Chen Ping again named Lu Jia Grand Palace Grandee. Lu Jia persuaded Wei Tuo to shed the yellow canopy and imperial rhetoric and accept parity with other feudal lords—exactly as the throne wished.

sentences.zh: 語在《南越傳》。

translations.literal: The account is given in the "Biography of Southern Yue."
translations.idiomatic: The particulars are recorded in the "Account of Southern Yue."

sentences.zh: 陸生竟以壽終。

translations.literal: Lu Sheng in the end died a natural death.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia lived out his years in peace.

sentences.zh: 硃建，楚人也。

translations.literal: Zhu Jian was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jian was a native of Chu.

sentences.zh: 故嘗為淮南王黥布相，有罪去，後復事布。

translations.literal: Formerly he had served as chancellor to King of Huainan Ying Bu; on account of a crime he departed, but later again served Bu.
translations.idiomatic: He had once been chancellor to Huainan king Ying Bu, left under a cloud, and later returned to Bu's service.

sentences.zh: 布欲反時，問建，建諫止之。

translations.literal: When Bu wished to rebel he consulted Jian; Jian remonstrated to stop him.
translations.idiomatic: When Bu plotted revolt he asked Zhu Jian, who urged him to abandon the scheme.

sentences.zh: 布不聽，聽梁父侯，遂反。

translations.literal: Bu did not listen; he listened to the Marquis of Liangfu, and thereupon rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Bu ignored him, followed the Marquis of Liangfu's counsel, and rose anyway.

sentences.zh: 漢既誅布，聞建諫之，高祖賜建號平原君，家徙長安。

translations.literal: After Han had executed Bu, hearing that Jian had remonstrated him, the High Ancestor bestowed on Jian the title Lord of Pingyuan and moved his household to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: After Han executed Bu, Gaozu learned Zhu Jian had tried to dissuade him; he ennobled Zhu Jian as Lord of Pingyuan and transferred his family to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 為人辯有口，刻廉剛直，行不苟合，義不取容。

translations.literal: As a man he was eloquent and had a ready tongue; he carved integrity and was sternly straight; in conduct he did not casually join others, and in matters of right he did not seek accommodation.
translations.idiomatic: He was quick-tongued, rigidly honest, unbending in action, and unwilling to trim principle for favor.

sentences.zh: 辟陽侯行不正，得幸呂太后，欲知建，建不肯見。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Piyang's conduct was not correct; he obtained favor with Empress Dowager Lü and wished to become acquainted with Jian, but Jian refused to see him.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Shen of Piyang, whose conduct was scandalous, enjoyed the dowager's favor; he sought an introduction to Zhu Jian, who would not receive him.

sentences.zh: 及建母死，貧未有以發喪，方假貣服具。

translations.literal: When Jian's mother died, he was poor and had no means to conduct the funeral; he was just then borrowing funeral goods on credit.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhu Jian's mother died he was too poor to bury her and was still borrowing mortuary goods.

sentences.zh: 陸賈素與建善，乃見辟陽侯，賀曰：「平原君母死。」

translations.literal: Lu Jia was on good terms with Jian; he therefore went to see the Marquis of Piyang and congratulated him, saying, "The Lord of Pingyuan's mother has died."
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia, who was Zhu Jian's friend, called on the marquis of Piyang to offer congratulations: "The Lord of Pingyuan's mother is dead."

sentences.zh: 辟陽侯曰：「平原君母死，何乃賀我？」

translations.literal: The Marquis of Piyang said, "The Lord of Pingyuan's mother has died—why do you congratulate me?"
translations.idiomatic: "His mother is dead—why congratulate me?"

sentences.zh: 陸生曰：「前日君侯欲知平原君，平原君義不知君，以其母故。

translations.literal: Lu said, "The other day, lord, you wished to know the Lord of Pingyuan; the Lord of Pingyuan, on principle of rightness, would not know you, because of his mother.
translations.idiomatic: "You wanted his friendship; out of duty to his mother he refused.

sentences.zh: 今其母死，君誠厚送喪，則彼為君死矣。」

translations.literal: Now that she has died, if you, lord, truly send generous funeral gifts, then he will die for you."
translations.idiomatic: Now she is gone—if you bury her handsomely, he will lay down his life for you in return."

sentences.zh: 辟陽侯乃奉百金稅，列侯貴人以辟陽侯故，往賻凡五百金。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Piyang thereupon presented a hundred in gold as burial gift; marquises and nobles, because of the Marquis of Piyang, went with condolence gifts totaling five hundred in gold.
translations.idiomatic: The marquis sent a hundred in gold as a mortuary gift; other peers, taking their cue from him, added condolences until the sum reached five hundred.

sentences.zh: 久之，人或毀辟陽侯，惠帝大怒，下吏，欲誅之。

translations.literal: After a long while someone slandered the Marquis of Piyang; Emperor Hui was greatly enraged, handed him to the clerks, and wished to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: In time someone denounced Shen to Emperor Hui, who flew into a rage, clapped him in chains, and prepared his execution.

sentences.zh: 太后慚，不可言。

translations.literal: The empress dowager was ashamed and could not speak.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager burned with shame and dared not speak for him.

sentences.zh: 大臣多害辟陽侯行，欲遂誅之。

translations.literal: The great ministers for the most part were harmed by the Marquis of Piyang's conduct and wished thereupon to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: Most ministers detested his conduct and pressed for immediate death.

sentences.zh: 辟陽侯困急，使人欲見建。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Piyang, in desperate straits, sent someone who wished to see Jian.
translations.idiomatic: Cornered, Shen sent for Zhu Jian.

sentences.zh: 建辭曰：「獄急，不敢見君。」

translations.literal: Jian declined, saying, "The prison case is urgent; I dare not see you, lord."
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jian refused: "The scandal is too hot—I cannot be seen with you."

sentences.zh: 建乃求見孝惠幸臣閎籍孺，說曰：「君所以得幸帝，天下莫不聞。

translations.literal: Jian then sought an audience with Emperor Hui's favored attendant Hong Jiru and persuaded him, saying, "The reason you, sir, have obtained favor with the emperor is something all under Heaven has heard.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jian went to Hong Jiru, the emperor's favorite, and said, "Everyone at court knows the sovereign dotes on you.

sentences.zh: 今辟陽侯幸太后而下吏，道路皆言君讒，欲殺之。

translations.literal: Now the Marquis of Piyang is favored by the empress dowager yet has been handed to the clerks; on the roads everyone says that you, sir, slandered him and wished to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Now Shen is the dowager's lover yet sits in jail, and every rumor says you whispered poison into the emperor's ear so he would kill Shen.

sentences.zh: 今日辟陽侯誅，且日太后含怒，亦誅君。

translations.literal: If today the Marquis of Piyang is executed, another day the empress dowager, harboring anger, will also execute you, sir."
translations.idiomatic: "If Shen dies today, the dowager's stored fury will take your head tomorrow."

sentences.zh: 君何不肉袒為辟陽侯言帝？

translations.literal: Why do you not bare your flesh to the waist and speak to the emperor for the Marquis of Piyang?
translations.idiomatic: "Strip to the waist and beg the emperor for his life.

sentences.zh: 帝聽君出辟陽侯，太后大驩。

translations.literal: If the emperor listens to you and releases the Marquis of Piyang, the empress dowager will be greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: If the emperor yields, the dowager will rejoice.

sentences.zh: 兩主俱幸君，君富貴益倍矣。」

translations.literal: When both sovereigns favor you, sir, your wealth and honor will be doubled."
translations.idiomatic: You will stand high with both rulers—and your fortune doubles."

sentences.zh: 於是閎籍孺大恐，從其計，言帝，帝果出辟陽侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon Hong Jiru was greatly afraid; he followed this plan, spoke to the emperor, and the emperor indeed released the Marquis of Piyang.
translations.idiomatic: Terror-stricken, Hong Jiru did as he was told, and the emperor spared the marquis.

sentences.zh: 辟陽侯之囚，欲見建，建不見，辟陽侯以為背之，大怒。

translations.literal: While the Marquis of Piyang was imprisoned he wished to see Jian; Jian would not see him, and the Marquis thought Jian had betrayed him and was greatly angry.
translations.idiomatic: While Shen still languished in prison he begged to see Zhu Jian; Zhu Jian stayed away, and Shen thought himself betrayed—he was furious.

sentences.zh: 乃其成功出之，大驚。

translations.literal: Only when the affair succeeded in releasing him was he greatly startled.
translations.idiomatic: When he walked free and learned how he had been saved, he was stunned.

sentences.zh: 呂太后崩，大臣誅諸呂，辟陽侯與諸呂至深，卒不誅。

translations.literal: When Empress Dowager Lü died, the great ministers executed the various Lüs; the Marquis of Piyang's ties to the Lüs were very deep, yet in the end he was not executed.
translations.idiomatic: After the dowager died the ministers purged the Lüs; Shen's bonds to them ran deep as blood, yet he alone survived.

sentences.zh: 計畫所以全者，皆陸生、平原君之力也。

translations.literal: The planning by which he was wholly preserved was entirely the work of Lu Sheng and the Lord of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia and the Lord of Pingyuan had engineered his deliverance.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，淮南厲王殺辟陽侯，以党諸呂故。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, the implacable King of Huainan killed the Marquis of Piyang because of his factional tie to the Lüs.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen the implacable king of Huainan slew Shen for abetting the Lü faction.

sentences.zh: 孝文聞其客硃建為其策，使吏捕欲治。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen heard that his retainer Zhu Jian had devised the king's strategy and sent clerks to arrest him, wishing to punish him.
translations.idiomatic: Wen learned Zhu Jian had plotted the murder and sent officers to seize him.

sentences.zh: 聞吏至門，建欲自殺。

translations.literal: When he heard that the clerks had reached the gate, Jian wished to kill himself.
translations.idiomatic: When the bailiffs reached his door Zhu Jian reached for the blade.

sentences.zh: 諸子及吏皆曰：「事未可知，何自殺為？」

translations.literal: His sons and the clerks all said, "The outcome is not yet knowable—why commit suicide?"
translations.idiomatic: His sons and the guards said, "The verdict is still unknown—why die now?"

sentences.zh: 建曰：「我死禍絕，不及乃身矣。」

translations.literal: Jian said, "If I die, the disaster is cut off and will not reach your persons."
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jian said, "If I die the matter ends with me; your skins stay whole."

sentences.zh: 遂自剄。

translations.literal: Thereupon he cut his own throat.
translations.idiomatic: He drew the knife across his own throat.

sentences.zh: 文帝聞而惜之，曰：「吾無殺建意也。」

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen heard this he pitied him and said, "I had no intention of killing Jian."
translations.idiomatic: Wen lamented: "I never meant to execute him."

sentences.zh: 乃召其子，拜為中大夫。

translations.literal: Thereupon he summoned Jian's son and appointed him Grand Palace Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: He summoned Zhu Jian's son and named him Grandee of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 使匈奴，單于無禮，罵單于，遂死匈奴中。

translations.literal: When sent to the Xiongnu the Chanyu was without courtesy; he reviled the Chanyu and thereupon died among the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: That son was posted to the Xiongnu; when the chanyu insulted him he hurled abuse back and died in the steppe.

sentences.zh: 婁敬，齊人也。

translations.literal: Lou Jing was a man of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Lou Jing came from Qi.

sentences.zh: 漢五年，戍隴西，過雒陽，高帝在焉。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Han he was on garrison duty in Longxi; passing through Luoyang, the High Ancestor was there.
translations.idiomatic: In Han year 5 he was bound for garrison in Longxi and passed through Luoyang, where Gaozu was camped.

sentences.zh: 敬脫挽輅，見齊人虞將軍曰：「臣願見上言便宜。」

translations.literal: Jing cast off the coupling-pole of his supply wagon, saw General Yu of Qi, and said, "Your subject wishes to see the sovereign and speak of what is advantageous."
translations.idiomatic: He dropped the traces of his baggage cart, found General Yu of Qi, and said, "I must see the emperor on a matter of policy."

sentences.zh: 虞將軍欲與鮮衣，敬曰：「臣衣帛，衣帛見，衣褐，衣褐見，不敢易衣。」

translations.literal: General Yu wished to give him fresh clothes; Jing said, "If your subject wears silk, he will appear in silk; if he wears coarse cloth, he will appear in coarse cloth—I dare not change my clothes."
translations.idiomatic: Yu offered court silks; Lou Jing refused: "If I wear silk I present myself in silk; if I wear homespun I come as I am—I will not change robes to curry favor."

sentences.zh: 虞將軍入言上，上召見，賜食。

translations.literal: General Yu entered and spoke to the sovereign; the sovereign summoned him for an audience and granted food.
translations.idiomatic: Yu relayed the request; Gaozu received him and had food brought.

sentences.zh: 漢七年，韓王信反，高帝自往擊。

translations.literal: In the seventh year of Han the King of Han Xin rebelled, and the High Ancestor personally went to attack him.
translations.idiomatic: In Han year 7 Han Xin of Han rebelled, and Gaozu marched in person against him.

sentences.zh: 至晉陽，聞信與匈奴欲擊漢，上大怒，使人使匈奴。

translations.literal: When he reached Jinyang he heard that Xin was with the Xiongnu and wished to strike Han; the sovereign was greatly enraged and sent men on mission to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: At Jinyang he learned Xin was colluding with the Xiongnu; he flew into a rage and dispatched spies to the steppe.

sentences.zh: 匈奴匿其壯士肥牛馬，徒見其老弱及羸畜。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu hid their strong warriors and fat oxen and horses, showing only their old and weak and their lean livestock.
translations.idiomatic: The nomads hid their warriors and prime herds, parading only the aged, the feeble, and starved beasts.

sentences.zh: 使者十蜚來，皆言匈奴易擊。

translations.literal: The envoys returned in quick succession ten times; all said the Xiongnu were easy to strike.
translations.idiomatic: Embassy after embassy reported the Xiongnu were easy prey.

sentences.zh: 上使劉敬復往使匈奴，還報曰：「兩國相擊，此宜誇矜見所長。

translations.literal: The sovereign sent Liu Jing again on mission to the Xiongnu; returning, he reported, "When two states strike each other, this is the time when each ought to boast and display its strengths.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu sent Lou Jing on another reconnaissance; he returned saying, "When two powers brace for war, each parades its strength.

sentences.zh: 今臣往，徒見羸胔老弱，此必欲見短，伏奇兵以爭利。

translations.literal: Now when your subject went, he saw only lean carcasses, old men, and the weak—they surely wish to show weakness and hide extraordinary troops to contend for advantage.
translations.idiomatic: I saw only gaunt men and broken-down herds—they mean to look feeble while masking strike forces for an ambush.

sentences.zh: 愚以為匈奴不可擊也。」

translations.literal: In this foolish one's opinion the Xiongnu cannot be attacked."
translations.idiomatic: In my humble view the Xiongnu must not be attacked."

sentences.zh: 是時漢兵以逾句注，三十餘萬眾，兵已業行。

translations.literal: At this time Han troops had already passed Gouzhu—more than three hundred thousand strong—and the army had already set out on the march.
translations.idiomatic: By then over three hundred thousand Han soldiers had crossed the Gouzhu pass; the host was already committed to the campaign.

sentences.zh: 上怒，罵敬曰：「齊虜！

translations.literal: The sovereign was enraged and reviled Jing, saying, "Qi captive!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exploded and cursed Lou Jing: "You Qi captive!

sentences.zh: 以舌得官，乃今妄言沮吾軍！」

translations.literal: You won office with your tongue, and now you recklessly speak to dampen the spirits of our army!"
translations.idiomatic: You talked your way into rank, and now you spread lies to stop my army cold!"

sentences.zh: 械系敬廣武。

translations.literal: They put him in fetters and bound him at Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: They clapped him in irons and held him under guard at Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 遂往，至平城，匈奴果出奇兵圍高帝白登，七日然後得解。

translations.literal: He still went forward; reaching Pingcheng, the Xiongnu indeed brought out concealed troops and surrounded the High Ancestor on Baideng; only after seven days did the siege lift.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu pressed on anyway. At Pingcheng the Xiongnu sprang their trap and ringed him on Baideng; seven days passed before he broke out.

sentences.zh: 高帝至廣武，赦敬，曰：「吾不用公言，以困平城。

translations.literal: When the High Ancestor reached Guangwu, he pardoned Jing and said, "I did not heed your, sir's, counsel and thereby became trapped at Pingcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Back at Guangwu Gaozu freed Lou Jing and said, "I ignored your warning and walked into the Pingcheng disaster.

sentences.zh: 吾已斬先使十輩言可擊者矣。」

translations.literal: I have already beheaded the ten successive parties of earlier envoys who said the Xiongnu could be attacked."
translations.idiomatic: I have already executed every earlier messenger who swore the nomads were easy prey."

sentences.zh: 乃封敬二千戶，為關內侯，號建信侯。

translations.literal: Thereupon he enfeoffed Jing with two thousand households as marquis within the passes, with the title Marquis Jianxin.
translations.idiomatic: He then enfeoffed Lou Jing at two thousand households as a marquis-within-the-passes under the style Marquis Who Builds Trust.

sentences.zh: 高帝罷平城歸，韓王信亡人胡。

translations.literal: After the High Ancestor withdrew from Pingcheng and returned, the King of Han Xin fled into the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu retreated from Pingcheng; Han Xin of Han defected into the steppe.

sentences.zh: 當是時，冒頓單于兵強，控弦四十萬騎，數若北邊。

translations.literal: At that time Modu the Chanyu's troops were strong—four hundred thousand bowmen on horseback—and repeatedly pressed the northern frontier.
translations.idiomatic: Modu Chanyu then fielded four hundred thousand mounted archers and raided the north without pause.

sentences.zh: 上患之，問敬。

translations.literal: The sovereign worried over this and questioned Jing.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor brooded over the threat and asked Lou Jing for a policy.

sentences.zh: 敬曰：「天下初定，士卒罷於兵革，未可以武服也。

translations.literal: Jing said, "The empire is newly settled; soldiers are weary of arms—it is not yet possible to subdue them by force.
translations.idiomatic: Lou Jing said, "The realm is barely pacified and the troops are spent—you cannot cow them with steel alone.

sentences.zh: 冒頓殺人父代立，妻群母，以力為威，未可以仁義說也。

translations.literal: Modu murdered his father to succeed, took his stepmothers to wife, and relies on brute force for awe—it is not yet possible to persuade him with benevolence and rightness.
translations.idiomatic: Modu killed his father for the throne, married his father's widows, and rules by terror—virtuous words will not move him.

sentences.zh: 獨可以計久遠子孫為臣耳，然陛下恐不能為。」

translations.literal: Only by a long-term stratagem that makes his descendants your subjects may it be done—yet Your Majesty may be unable to carry it out."
translations.idiomatic: You can only bind him with a design that turns his heirs into vassals—and even that asks something hard of you."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「誠可，何為不能！

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "If it can truly be done, why could I not do it!
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu said, "If the thing is sound, why should I shrink from it!

sentences.zh: 顧為奈何？」

translations.literal: Only, what is to be done?"
translations.idiomatic: What exactly do you propose?"

sentences.zh: 敬曰：「陛下誠能以適長公主妻單于，厚奉遺之，彼知漢女送厚，蠻夷必慕，以為閼氏，生子必為太子，代單于。

translations.literal: Jing said, "If Your Majesty can truly marry the eldest legitimate princess to the Chanyu with rich dowry and gifts, he, knowing how generously Han sends its daughter, the barbarians will surely admire her and make her senior consort; the son born will surely be heir and succeed as Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Lou Jing answered, "Send your eldest princess by the empress to the chanyu with a lavish bride-price. He will prize a Han princess and make her chief wife; her son will be heir apparent and someday chanyu himself.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why would that work?

sentences.zh: 貪漢重幣。

translations.literal: Because they covet Han's heavy treasure.
translations.idiomatic: Because they hunger for Han gold and silks.

sentences.zh: 陛下以歲時漢所餘彼所鮮數問遺，使辯士風喻以禮節。

translations.literal: Your Majesty, at the yearly seasons, should repeatedly send gifts of what Han has in surplus and they lack, and have persuasive men instruct them in ritual propriety.
translations.idiomatic: Each year shower them with what we have in plenty and they lack, and let eloquent envoys tutor them in courtesy.

sentences.zh: 冒頓在，固為子婿；

translations.literal: While Modu lives, he is naturally your son-in-law;
translations.idiomatic: Modu alive is your son-in-law;

sentences.zh: 死，外孫為單于。

translations.literal: when he dies, your maternal grandson will be Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: when he dies your grandson sits on the throne.

sentences.zh: 豈曾聞孫敢與大父亢禮哉？

translations.literal: Has one ever heard of a grandson who dares match ceremonies with his grandfather?
translations.idiomatic: What grandson would face his grandfather as an equal?

sentences.zh: 可毋戰以漸臣也。

translations.literal: Thus without battle they may gradually be made subjects.
translations.idiomatic: You can turn them into vassals without drawing a bow.

sentences.zh: 若陛下不能遣長公主，而令宗室及後宮詐稱公主，彼亦知不肯貴近，無益也。」

translations.literal: If Your Majesty cannot dispatch the eldest princess but has some girl of the imperial clan or the rear palace falsely titled princess, they will know and refuse to honor or draw near her—it will be useless."
translations.idiomatic: If you palm off a lesser daughter of the house or a palace maid as the princess, they will sniff out the fraud, scorn the match, and the whole scheme fails."

sentences.zh: 高帝曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu said, "Agreed."

sentences.zh: 欲遣長公主。

translations.literal: He wished to send the eldest princess.
translations.idiomatic: He prepared to send the true eldest princess.

sentences.zh: 呂後泣曰：「妾唯以一太子、一女，奈何棄之匈奴！」

translations.literal: Empress Lü wept and said, "Your handmaid has only this one heir apparent and this one daughter—how can you abandon her to the Xiongnu!"
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü wept: "I have but one crown prince and one daughter—do not cast her to the nomads!"

sentences.zh: 上竟不能遣長公主，而取家人子為公主，妻單于。

translations.literal: In the end the sovereign could not send the eldest princess; he took a servant girl of the household as princess and gave her to the Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu yielded to her tears, picked a foster daughter from the inner court, and sent her north as the princess.

sentences.zh: 使敬往結和親約。

translations.literal: He sent Jing to conclude the marriage-alliance treaty.
translations.idiomatic: He dispatched Lou Jing to seal the heqin pact.

sentences.zh: 敬從匈奴來，因言「匈奴河南白羊、樓煩王，去長安近者七百里，輕騎一日一夕可以至。

translations.literal: When Jing returned from the Xiongnu he therefore said, "The Báiyáng and Loufan kings of the Xiongnu south of the river are seven hundred li from Chang'an at the nearest point; light cavalry can reach you in a day and a night.
translations.idiomatic: On his return from the steppe Lou Jing warned: "The Báiyáng and Loufan kings south of the Yellow River lie within seven hundred li of Chang'an—mounted raiders could strike in a day and a night.

sentences.zh: 秦中新破，少民，地肥饒，可益實。

translations.literal: The central region is newly shattered, short of people, yet the land is fat and rich—it may be further settled.
translations.idiomatic: Guanzhong is still depopulated from the wars, yet the soil is rich—fill it with people.

sentences.zh: 夫諸侯初起時，非齊諸田，楚昭、屈、景莫與。

translations.literal: When the feudal lords first rose, none could compare with the Tian of Qi or the Zhao, Qu, and Jing of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: When the rebellion began, no line rivaled the Tian of Qi or the Zhao, Qu, and Jing houses of Chu.

sentences.zh: 今陛下雖都關中，實少人。

translations.literal: Now although Your Majesty has established your capital in Guanzhong, in truth there are few people.
translations.idiomatic: You rule from Guanzhong, but the basin is thinly peopled.

sentences.zh: 北近胡冠，東有六國強族，一日有變，陛下亦未得安枕而臥也。

translations.literal: To the north you border the Hu raiders; to the east stand the powerful clans of the Six States—should trouble arise in a single day, Your Majesty will still not be able to lay your head on the pillow in peace.
translations.idiomatic: Hu horsemen loom to the north; the old great-clan networks of the Six States lie to the east—one spark and you will not sleep soundly.

sentences.zh: 臣願陛下徙齊諸田，楚昭、屈、景、燕、趙、韓、魏後，及豪傑名家，且實關中。

translations.literal: Your subject begs Your Majesty to move the Tian of Qi, the Zhao, Qu, and Jing of Chu, the descendants of Yan, Zhao, Han, and Wei, and the famous houses of heroes and champions, and thereby populate Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: I urge you to transplant the Tian of Qi, the Zhao, Qu, and Jing of Chu, the surviving houses of Yan, Zhao, Han, and Wei, and every notable clan into Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 無事，可以備胡；

translations.literal: In ordinary times they can guard against the Hu;
translations.idiomatic: In quiet years they stiffen the frontier against the Hu;

sentences.zh: 諸侯有變，亦足率以東伐。

translations.literal: if the feudal lords have a revolt, they are also enough to lead an expedition eastward.
translations.idiomatic: if the lords rebel, they give you a ready host to march east.

sentences.zh: 此強本弱末之術也。」

translations.literal: This is the method of strengthening the root and weakening the branches."
translations.idiomatic: That is how you thicken the trunk and prune the branches."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「善。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "Good."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Well said."

sentences.zh: 乃使劉敬徙所言關中十餘萬口。

translations.literal: Thereupon he had Liu Jing move the more than one hundred thousand persons spoken of into Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered Lou Jing to resettle the hundred thousand-plus households he had named into the capital region.

sentences.zh: 叔孫通

translations.literal: Shusun Tong
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong

sentences.zh: 叔孫通，薛人也。

translations.literal: Shusun Tong was a man of Xue.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong came from Xue.

sentences.zh: 秦時以文學征，待詔博士。

translations.literal: In Qin times he was summoned for literary accomplishment and awaited edicts as an Erudite.
translations.idiomatic: Under Qin he earned a summons on literary merit and held a reserve appointment among the court erudites.

sentences.zh: 數歲，陳勝起，二世召博士諸儒生問曰：「楚戍卒攻蘄入陳，於公何如？」

translations.literal: Several years later, when Chen Sheng rose, the Second Emperor summoned the erudites and various Confucians and asked, "Chu garrison conscripts attacked Qi and entered Chen—what do you gentlemen make of it?"
translations.idiomatic: Years later Chen Sheng revolted. Ershi summoned the erudites and asked what it meant that Chu conscripts had stormed Qi and seized Chen.

sentences.zh: 博士諸生三十餘人前曰：「人臣無將，將則反，罪死無赦。

translations.literal: More than thirty erudites and students stepped forward and said, "A minister must not command troops; if he commands troops he rebels—the crime is death without pardon.
translations.idiomatic: Thirty-odd scholars stepped up: "No subject may take command; to take arms is treason deserving death without pardon.

sentences.zh: 願陛下急發兵擊之。」

translations.literal: We beg Your Majesty urgently to dispatch troops to strike them."
translations.idiomatic: Send an army at once to crush them."

sentences.zh: 二世怒，作色。

translations.literal: The Second Emperor was angry and changed color.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi flushed with rage.

sentences.zh: 通前曰：「諸生言皆非。

translations.literal: Tong stepped forward and said, "What the various students have said is all wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong advanced: "Every word they spoke is wrong.

sentences.zh: 夫天下為一家，毀郡縣城，鑠其兵，視天下弗復用。

translations.literal: All under Heaven has become one family—they have destroyed the walls of the commanderies and prefectures and melted down their weapons, showing that they will never again be used in the empire.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is one house now: the Qin have razed local fortifications and smelted the weapons—there is no more use for arms.

sentences.zh: 且明主在上，法令具於下，吏人人奉職，四方輻輳，安有反者！

translations.literal: Moreover, with a bright ruler above and complete laws below, every clerk fulfilling his duty and the four quarters converging like spokes to a hub—how could there be rebels!
translations.idiomatic: A clear-sighted sovereign sits on high, statutes run to every yamen, officials do their rounds, and the corners of the empire pay court—where would rebels come from?

sentences.zh: 此特群盜鼠竊狗盜，何足置齒牙間哉？

translations.literal: These are merely a pack of bandits stealing like rats and dogs—how are they worth mentioning between teeth?
translations.idiomatic: These are mice and cur-thieves beneath notice.

sentences.zh: 郡守尉今捕誅，何足憂？」

translations.literal: The commandery governor and commandant will capture and execute them—what is there to worry about?"
translations.idiomatic: County commandants will hunt them down—why lose sleep?"

sentences.zh: 二世喜，盡問諸生，諸生或言反，或言盜。

translations.literal: The Second Emperor was pleased, questioned all the students; some spoke of rebellion, some of banditry.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi brightened, polled the hall: some called it revolt, some brigandage.

sentences.zh: 於是二世令御史按諸生言反者下吏，非所宜言。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Second Emperor ordered the imperial clerks to investigate those who had spoken of rebellion and hand them to the clerks—not speech that ought to have been uttered.
translations.idiomatic: He told the censors to jail everyone who had said "rebellion"—disloyal speech.

sentences.zh: 諸生言盜者皆罷之。

translations.literal: Those who had spoken of banditry were all dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who had said "bandits" was sent home.

sentences.zh: 乃賜通帛二十匹，衣一襲，拜為博士，通已出，反舍，諸生曰：「生何言之諛也？」

translations.literal: He then presented Tong with twenty bolts of silk and one full suit of clothes and appointed him Erudite; when Tong had gone out and returned to his lodging, the students said, "Sir, how could your words have been so flattering?"
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong won twenty bolts of silk, a new robe, and a renewed erudite title. Back at his quarters his fellows hissed, "How could you crawl like that?"

sentences.zh: 通曰：「公不知，我幾不免虎口！」

translations.literal: Tong said, "You gentlemen do not understand—I nearly failed to escape the tiger's mouth!"
translations.idiomatic: He shot back, "You nearly watched me fed to a tiger—you have no idea."

sentences.zh: 乃亡去之薛，薛已降楚矣。

translations.literal: Thereupon he fled and went to Xue, but Xue had already surrendered to Chu.
translations.idiomatic: He bolted for Xue, only to find Xue already under Chu's flag.

sentences.zh: 及項梁之薛，通從之。

translations.literal: When Xiang Liang came to Xue, Tong followed him.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Liang marched on Xue, Shusun Tong joined him.

sentences.zh: 敗定陶，從懷王。

translations.literal: After the defeat at Dingtao he followed King Huai.
translations.idiomatic: After the disaster at Dingtao he attached himself to King Huai.

sentences.zh: 懷王為義帝，徙長沙，通留事項王，漢二年，漢王從五諸侯入彭城，通降漢王。

translations.literal: When Huai became the Righteous Emperor and was moved to Changsha, Tong remained to serve King Xiang; in the second year of Han the Han king entered Pengcheng with the five feudal lords, and Tong submitted to the Han king.
translations.idiomatic: Huai became the Righteous Emperor and was banished to Changsha; Shusun Tong stayed with Xiang Yu. In Han year 2 the King of Han swept into Pengcheng with five allied kings, and Shusun Tong crossed to Han.

sentences.zh: 通儒服，漢王憎之，乃變其服，服短衣，楚制。

translations.literal: Tong wore Confucian robes; the Han king detested this, so he changed his garments and wore short clothes in the Chu style.
translations.idiomatic: He arrived in scholar's robes; Liu Bang hated the look, so he switched to short Chu jackets.

sentences.zh: 漢王喜。

translations.literal: The Han king was pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han liked that better.

sentences.zh: 通之降漢，從弟子百餘人，然無所進，剸言諸故群盜壯士進之。

translations.literal: When Tong submitted to Han he had more than a hundred disciples following him, yet he promoted none of them; he only spoke of former bandit bravos and had them advanced.
translations.idiomatic: He brought a hundred disciples yet recommended none of them—only old bandit chiefs won promotion.
