sentences.zh: 弟子皆曰：「事先生數年，幸得從降漢，今不進臣等，剸言大猾，何也？」

translations.literal: The disciples all said, "We have served the master for several years, and were fortunate enough to follow him in submitting to Han; now he does not advance us but speaks only of great scoundrels—why?"
translations.idiomatic: His students protested: "We studied under you for years and followed you to Han—why push cutthroats ahead of us?"

sentences.zh: 通乃謂曰：「漢王方蒙矢石爭天下，諸生寧能鬥乎？

translations.literal: Tong thereupon said to them, "The Han king is just now braving arrows and stones to contend for all under Heaven—can you, gentlemen, fight?
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong answered, "Your patron is dodging arrows to win an empire—can any of you hold a spear?

sentences.zh: 故先言斬將搴旗之士。

translations.literal: Therefore I first speak of men who behead generals and seize banners.
translations.idiomatic: So I start with men who can take heads and haul standards.

sentences.zh: 諸生且待我，我不忘矣。」

translations.literal: You gentlemen wait for me a while—I shall not forget you."
translations.idiomatic: Wait your turn—I have not forgotten you."

sentences.zh: 漢王拜通為博士，號稷嗣君。

translations.literal: The Han king appointed Tong Erudite with the title Lord Who Succeeds the Ji.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han named him erudite with the style Lord Who Inherits the Altar of Ji.

sentences.zh: 漢王已並天下，諸侯共尊為皇帝于定陶，通就其儀號。

translations.literal: After the Han king had united all under Heaven, the feudal lords together honored him as emperor at Dingtao, and Tong fixed his titles and forms of address.
translations.idiomatic: When the lords acclaimed Liu Bang emperor at Dingtao, Shusun Tong drafted titles and protocol.

sentences.zh: 高帝悉去秦儀法，為簡易。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor abolished all Qin ritual and law and made things simple and easy.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu junked Qin court etiquette and kept things rough-and-ready.

sentences.zh: 群臣飲爭功，醉或妄呼，拔劍擊柱，上患之。

translations.literal: The group of ministers drank and disputed their merits; when drunk some wildly shouted, drew swords, and struck the pillars—the sovereign worried over this.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers brawled drunk over who deserved what, drew steel on the palace columns—the throne was alarmed.

sentences.zh: 通知上亦厭之，說上曰：「夫儒者難與進取，可與守成。

translations.literal: Tong knew the sovereign also was weary of this; he persuaded the sovereign, saying, "Confucians are hard to use for seizing territory, but they can be used for preserving what is already won.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong saw the danger and said, "Scholars are poor shock troops but excellent stewards of a settled throne.

sentences.zh: 臣願征魯諸生，與臣弟子共起朝儀。」

translations.literal: Your subject begs to summon the scholars of Lu and, together with my disciples, to institute court ritual."
translations.idiomatic: Let me summon the Ru of Lu and train your court in proper ceremony."

sentences.zh: 高帝曰：「得無難乎？」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "Will it not be difficult?"
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu asked, "Isn't that impossibly heavy?"

sentences.zh: 通曰：「五帝異樂，三王不同禮。

translations.literal: Tong said, "The Five Emperors had different music; the Three Kings had different ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong replied, "The Five Emperors sang different tunes; the Three Kings wore different rites.

sentences.zh: 禮者，因時世人情為之節文者也。

translations.literal: Ritual is that which, according to the age and the feelings of the world, sets pattern and ornament.
translations.idiomatic: Ceremony is the garment cut to fit the age and the temper of the people.

sentences.zh: 故夏、殷、周禮所因損益可知者，謂不相復也。

translations.literal: Therefore what the rituals of Xia, Yin, and Zhou took up and cast aside can be known—they mean that they did not repeat one another.
translations.idiomatic: That is why Xia, Shang, and Zhou each rewrote the book instead of Xeroxing their predecessors.

sentences.zh: 臣願頗采古禮與秦儀雜就之。」

translations.literal: Your subject wishes to take somewhat from ancient ritual and mix it with Qin ceremony to complete it."
translations.idiomatic: I propose to blend classical forms with what survives of Qin court drill."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「可試為之，令易知，度吾所能行為之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "You may try to make it; order it easy to understand, and gauge it by what I am able to perform."
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu said, "Try—but keep it plain enough that I can actually do it."

sentences.zh: 於是通使征魯諸生三十餘人。

translations.literal: Thereupon Tong sent to summon more than thirty scholars of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: He sent to Lu for thirty-odd classicists.

sentences.zh: 魯有兩生不肯行，曰：「公所事者且十主，皆面腴親貴。

translations.literal: In Lu two scholars refused to go, saying, "The lords you have served are nearly ten in number, and all have been men with plump faces who were intimate with the powerful.
translations.idiomatic: Two old men refused: "You have curried nearly ten masters, every one a well-fed favorite of the mighty.

sentences.zh: 今天下初定，死者未葬，傷者未起，又欲起禮樂。

translations.literal: Now all under Heaven is newly settled; the dead are not yet buried, the wounded are not yet risen, and again you wish to raise ritual and music.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is still a battlefield of unburied dead and crippled veterans—and you want to stage music and dance?

sentences.zh: 禮樂所由起，百年積德而後可興也。

translations.literal: The rise of ritual and music requires a hundred years of accumulated virtue before it can flourish.
translations.idiomatic: Rites and music need a century of virtue before they belong in public life.

sentences.zh: 吾不忍為公所為。

translations.literal: We cannot bear to do what you, sir, do.
translations.idiomatic: We will not soil ourselves with your errand.

sentences.zh: 公所為不合古，吾不行。

translations.literal: What you, sir, do does not match antiquity; we will not go.
translations.idiomatic: Your scheme is not the way of the sages.

sentences.zh: 公往矣，毋汙我！」

translations.literal: Sir, go—do not defile us!"
translations.idiomatic: Go—and leave us out of it!"

sentences.zh: 通笑曰：「若真鄙儒，不知時變。」

translations.literal: Tong laughed and said, "You are truly vulgar Confucians—you do not know how to change with the times."
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong laughed: "Pedants—you cannot read the hour on the clock."

sentences.zh: 遂與所征三十人西，及上左右為學者與其弟子百餘人為綿蕞野外。

translations.literal: He then went west with the thirty he had summoned, together with the scholars at the sovereign's left and right and his disciples—more than a hundred—and tied ropes and reeds in the wilds for practice.
translations.idiomatic: He marched west with his thirty recruits, the capital scholars who joined him, and his disciples—over a hundred men—and laid out straw markers in the fields to rehearse.

sentences.zh: 習之月餘，通曰：「上可試觀。」

translations.literal: After more than a month of practice Tong said, "The sovereign may try viewing it."
translations.idiomatic: After a month he told Gaozu, "You can inspect a dress rehearsal."

sentences.zh: 上使行禮，曰：「吾能為此。」

translations.literal: The sovereign ordered them to perform the ritual and said, "I can do this."
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu watched a run-through and said, "I can manage that."

sentences.zh: 乃令群臣習肄，會十月。

translations.literal: He then ordered the group of ministers to practice drill, to assemble in the tenth month.
translations.idiomatic: He told the ministers to drill until the tenth-month audience.

sentences.zh: 漢七年，長樂宮成，諸侯群臣朝十月。

translations.literal: In the seventh year of Han the Changle Palace was completed; the feudal lords and the group of ministers attended court in the tenth month.
translations.idiomatic: In Han year 7 Changle Palace was finished; lords and ministers gathered for the winter court.

sentences.zh: 儀：先平明，謁者治禮，引以次入殿門。

translations.literal: The ritual: first, at the time when dawn clears, the herald regulates the ritual and leads them in order through the hall gates.
translations.idiomatic: The order of the day: before dawn broke, chamberlains set the ritual in motion and led each rank through the palace gates in turn.

sentences.zh: 廷中陳車騎戍卒衛官，設兵，張旗志。

translations.literal: In the courtyard were arrayed chariots, horses, garrison soldiers, and guards; weapons were set out and flags spread.
translations.idiomatic: The courtyard filled with chariots, pickets, arms, and streaming banners.

sentences.zh: 傳曰「趨」。

translations.literal: The transmission called, "Hasten!"
translations.idiomatic: Callers barked, "Quick step!"

sentences.zh: 殿下郎中俠陛，陛數百人。

translations.literal: Below the hall, gentlemen-attendants flanked the steps; several hundred stood on the stairways.
translations.idiomatic: Gentlemen of the palace flanked the stairs—hundreds on the terraces.

sentences.zh: 功臣、列侯、諸將軍、軍吏以次陳西方，東鄉；

translations.literal: Meritorious ministers, marquises, the various generals, and military officers were arrayed in order on the west, facing east;
translations.idiomatic: Merit marquises and generals formed up on the west, facing east;

sentences.zh: 文官丞相以下陳東方，西鄉。

translations.literal: civil officials from the chancellor downward were arrayed on the east, facing west.
translations.idiomatic: civil officers from the chancellor down lined the east, facing west.

sentences.zh: 大行設九賓，臚句傳。

translations.literal: The Director of Grand Processions arranged the nine ranks of guests and relayed each formulaic phrase down the line of heralds.
translations.idiomatic: The grand usher staged the nine-tier guest array and passed the litany of titles from herald to herald.

sentences.zh: 於是皇帝輦出房，百官執戟傳警，引諸侯王以下至吏六百石以次奉賀。

translations.literal: Thereupon the imperial palanquin emerged from the inner chamber; the hundred officials, halberds in hand, passed the alarm in relay and led the kings of the feudal lords on down to clerks of six hundred bushels of grain in salary to offer congratulations in strict order.
translations.idiomatic: Then Gaozu's litter rolled from the private apartments; guards with halberds called the watches and shepherded every rank from the kings to the six-hundred-bushel officials forward to pay court in sequence.

sentences.zh: 自諸侯王以下莫不震恐肅敬。

translations.literal: From the kings of the feudal lords downward none failed to tremble with awe and show solemn respect.
translations.idiomatic: From the royal enfeoffees downward every man stood rigid with awe.

sentences.zh: 至禮畢，盡伏，置法酒。

translations.literal: When the ritual was finished, all prostrated themselves; regulated ceremonial wine was set out.
translations.idiomatic: When the rite closed the hall dropped to its knees; stewards poured the measured toast.

sentences.zh: 諸侍坐殿下皆伏抑首，以尊卑次起上壽。

translations.literal: All those seated in attendance below the dais pressed their foreheads down, then according to rank of honor and humility rose in turn to pledge the sovereign long life.
translations.idiomatic: Courtiers along the floorboards kept their brows to the mats, then rose by seniority to drink the emperor's health.

sentences.zh: 觴九行，謁者言「罷酒」。

translations.literal: After nine rounds of toasting cups, the herald announced, "The wine is dismissed."
translations.idiomatic: Nine passes of the cup ended before the usher intoned, "The banquet is closed."

sentences.zh: 御史執法舉不如儀者輒引去。

translations.literal: The imperial clerks who enforced the statutes singled out anyone whose deportment fell short of the prescribed forms and at once led them away.
translations.idiomatic: Censors marked any slip in deportment and had the offender marched out immediately.

sentences.zh: 竟朝置酒，無敢讙嘩失禮者。

translations.literal: Through the entire court session with wine served, none dared shout in disorder or lapse from ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Not a single drunk squabble marred the whole audience.

sentences.zh: 於是高帝曰：「吾乃今日知為皇帝之貴也！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the High Ancestor said, "Only today do I know the nobility of being emperor!"
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu burst out, "Not until this hour did I understand what the throne is worth!"

sentences.zh: 拜通為奉常，賜金五百斤。

translations.literal: He appointed Tong Director of Imperial Sacrifices (Fengchang) and presented him with five hundred jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: He promoted Shusun Tong to Director of Imperial Sacrifices and poured five hundred jin of gold into his lap.

sentences.zh: 通因進曰：「諸弟子儒生隨臣久矣，與共為儀，願陛下官之。」

translations.literal: Tong thereupon advanced and said, "The disciples and scholars have long followed your subject and together with him performed this ritual; I beg Your Majesty to grant them office."
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong added, "These students rehearsed the ceremony with me—grant them posts."

sentences.zh: 高帝悉以為郎。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor made them all gentlemen of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu named every one of them court gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 通出，皆以五百金賜諸生。

translations.literal: When Tong went out, he presented all five hundred jin of gold to the various students.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong walked out and shared the imperial gold with his entire school.

sentences.zh: 諸生乃喜曰：「叔孫生聖人，知當世務。」

translations.literal: The students thereupon joyfully said, "Master Shusun is a sage—he understands the business of the age."
translations.idiomatic: They cheered, "Master Shusun reads the times like a sage."

sentences.zh: 九年，高帝徙通為太子太傅。

translations.literal: In the ninth year the High Ancestor transferred Tong to be Grand Tutor to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: In the ninth year Gaozu moved him to grand tutor of the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 十二年，高帝欲以趙王如意易太子，通諫曰：「昔者晉獻公以驪姬故，廢太子，立奚齊，晉國亂者數十年，為天下笑。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year the High Ancestor wished to replace the heir apparent with Zhao Prince Ruyi; Tong remonstrated, saying, "In former times Duke Xian of Jin, on account of Lady Li Ji, cast aside the crown prince and enthroned Xi Qi, and the state of Jin was in turmoil for several decades and became the laughingstock of the empire.
translations.idiomatic: In year twelve Gaozu meant to swap the lawful heir for Prince Ruyi of Zhao. Shusun Tong objected: "Duke Xian of Jin listened to Lady Li Ji, cashiered the crown prince, and raised Xi Qi—decades of civil war and a joke to the world.

sentences.zh: 秦以不早定扶蘇，故亥詐立，自使滅祀，此陛下所親見。

translations.literal: Qin because it did not early settle the succession on Fusu, therefore Huhai falsely seized the throne and thereby brought about the extinction of its sacrifices—this is what Your Majesty has witnessed with your own eyes.
translations.idiomatic: Qin never fixed the succession on Fusu, so Huhai forged an edict and cut his own ancestral line short—you watched that lesson yourself.

sentences.zh: 今太子仁孝，天下皆聞之；

translations.literal: Now the crown prince is humane and filial; all under Heaven has heard of it;
translations.idiomatic: The heir you have is humane and filial; the whole realm knows it;

sentences.zh: 呂後與陛下攻苦食啖，其可背哉！

translations.literal: Empress Lü endured hardship and shared coarse fare with Your Majesty—how can you turn your back on her!
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü shared your bitter campaigns—would you cast her aside!

sentences.zh: 陛下必欲廢適而立少，臣願先伏誅，以頸血污地。」

translations.literal: If Your Majesty must cast aside the heir born of the principal consort and enthrone the younger, your subject begs first to lie executed and let the blood of his neck soil the ground.
translations.idiomatic: If you insist on displacing the rightful heir for the younger boy, strike off my head first and let my blood pool on these stones.

sentences.zh: 高帝曰：「公罷矣，吾特戲耳。」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "Sir, desist—I was only jesting."
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu waved him down: "Enough talk—it was only a jest."

sentences.zh: 通曰：「太子天下本，本壹搖天下震動，奈何以天下戲！」

translations.literal: Tong said, "The crown prince is the root of all under Heaven; once the root is shaken, all under Heaven trembles—how can you jest with all under Heaven!"
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong shot back, "The heir is the taproot of the state—jar it once and the realm quakes; the empire is no subject for jokes!"

sentences.zh: 高帝曰：「吾聽公。」

translations.literal: The High Ancestor said, "I heed you, sir."
translations.idiomatic: "I accept your counsel."

sentences.zh: 及上置酒，見留侯所招客從太子入見，上遂無易太子志矣。

translations.literal: When the sovereign held a banquet and saw the guests Marquis of Liu had recruited following the crown prince in to audience, the sovereign then had no further intention of replacing the crown prince.
translations.idiomatic: Later, at a palace feast, Gaozu watched Lord Zhang's handpicked retainers file in behind the heir—and the whim to replace him died on the spot.

sentences.zh: 高帝崩，孝惠即位，乃謂通曰：「先帝園陵寢廟，群臣莫習。」

translations.literal: After the High Ancestor died and Emperor Hui assumed the throne, he said to Tong, "The park tombs, sleeping halls, and shrines of the former emperor—none of the group of ministers is practiced in them."
translations.idiomatic: When Gaozu died and Emperor Hui ascended, he told Shusun Tong, "No one in court knows how to serve Father's mausoleum shrines."

sentences.zh: 徙通為奉常，定宗廟儀法。

translations.literal: He transferred Tong to be Director of Imperial Sacrifices and fixed the ritual forms for the ancestral temples.
translations.idiomatic: He restored Shusun Tong to Director of Sacrifices to codify the ancestral cult.

sentences.zh: 乃稍定漢諸儀法，皆通所論著也。

translations.literal: Thereupon Han's various ritual forms were gradually settled—all were matters that Tong discussed and wrote.
translations.idiomatic: Piece by piece the Han court ritual took shape, and every clause bore Shusun Tong's hand.

sentences.zh: 惠帝為東朝長樂宮，及間往，數蹕煩民，作復道，方築武庫南，通奏事，因請間，曰：「陛下何自築復道高帝寢，衣冠月出遊高廟？

translations.literal: Emperor Hui held eastern audiences at Changle Palace; when he went there from time to time, the repeated imperial road-clearings troubled the people, so he built a covered gallery, and just as it was being constructed south of the armory, Tong presented a memorial and seized the chance to request a private audience, saying, "Why would Your Majesty personally build a covered passage above the High Emperor's resting hall along which the caps and gowns are taken out monthly to tour the High Temple?
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Hui heard cases from Changle in the east; the constant cordons for his visits vexed the capital, so he threw an elevated walk across the route—right over the spirit road south of the arsenal. Shusun Tong filed a routine report, then asked for a private word: "Why pave a skyway across the path Gaozu's robes and crown travel each month to the high shrine?

sentences.zh: 子孫奈何乘宗廟道上行哉！」

translations.literal: How can descendants ride along the road of the ancestral shrines above!"
translations.idiomatic: Would you have a son stride the sacred lane reserved for the shrines!"

sentences.zh: 惠帝懼，曰：「急壞之。」

translations.literal: Emperor Hui was frightened and said, "Hurry and tear it down."
translations.idiomatic: Hui blanched: "Rip it out tonight."

sentences.zh: 通曰：「人主無過舉。

translations.literal: Tong said, "The ruler of men commits no misstep.
translations.idiomatic: Shusun Tong said, "A Son of Heaven never errs in public.

sentences.zh: 今已作，百姓皆知之矣。

translations.literal: Now it is already built and the common people all know of it.
translations.idiomatic: The gallery already stands; every ward in the capital knows you raised it.

sentences.zh: 願陛下為原廟渭北，衣冠月出遊之，益廣宗廟，大孝之本。」

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to establish distant shrines north of the Wei so that the caps and gowns may go out monthly to tour them—thereby extending the ancestral temples and laying the root of great filial piety."
translations.idiomatic: Build auxiliary shrines north of the Wei for the monthly procession instead—broaden the ancestral cult; that is the marrow of true filial piety."

sentences.zh: 上乃詔有司立原廟。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon ordered the responsible offices to establish the distant shrines.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor commanded the proper bureaus to raise the satellite temples.

sentences.zh: 惠帝常出游離宮，通曰：「古者有春嘗果，方今櫻桃熟，可獻，願陛下出，因取櫻桃獻宗廟。」

translations.literal: Emperor Hui often went out touring detached palaces; Tong said, "The ancients tasted the fruits of spring; now the cherries are ripe and may be offered—I beg Your Majesty to go out and thereby gather cherries to present at the ancestral temple."
translations.idiomatic: When Hui kept holidaying at outlying palaces, Shusun Tong said, "The rites call for spring fruit—cherries are ripe; let this outing furnish the first offering for the altars."

sentences.zh: 上許之。

translations.literal: The sovereign assented.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 諸果獻由此興。

translations.literal: The presentation of various fruits arose from this.
translations.idiomatic: Seasonal fruit offerings to the shrines began with that counsel.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：高祖以征伐定天下，而縉紳之徒聘其知辯，並成大業。

translations.literal: The appraiser says: The High Ancestor settled the empire by campaigns, yet gentry in sashes and tallies deployed their knowledge and eloquence and together completed the great enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: Gaozu hammered out the realm on campaign, but silk-girded scholars lent wit and rhetoric to seal the founding.

sentences.zh: 語曰：「廊廟之枝材一木之材，帝王之功非一士之略」，信哉！

translations.literal: The saying runs, "The crossbeams of the temple hall are not timber from a single tree; the achievement of emperors and kings is not the strategy of one knight"—how true!
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb has it, "No single log roofs the state hall; no lone strategist builds an imperial age"—and how true that is!

sentences.zh: 劉敬脫挽輅而建金城之安，叔孫通舍枹鼓而立一王之儀，遇其時也。

translations.literal: Liu Jing cast off the traces of his supply cart and secured the peace of the metal-walled frontier; Shusun Tong set aside drum and mallet and raised the ritual of a single supreme king—they met their moment.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jing dropped his wagon traces and forged the armored peace of the northern line; Shusun Tong traded the war drum for the choreography of one Son of Heaven—they seized the hour fate offered.

sentences.zh: 酈生自匿監門，待主然後出，猶不免鼎鑊。

translations.literal: Li Sheng hid himself as village gatekeeper and waited for his lord before emerging, yet still could not avoid the cauldron and boiling.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shiqi lurked at the hamlet gate until the right patron appeared—yet the cauldron still claimed him.

sentences.zh: 硃建始名廉直，既距辟陽，不終其節，亦以喪身。

translations.literal: Zhu Jian at first bore a reputation for carved integrity and stern uprightness; once he had dealings with the Marquis of Piyang he did not carry his integrity through to the end and thereby lost his life.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jian began as a byword for icy honor; entanglement with Marquis Shen broke the arc of his principles and cost him his life.

sentences.zh: 陸賈位止大夫，致仕諸呂，不受憂責，從容平、勃之間，附會將相以強社稷，身名俱榮，其最優乎！

translations.literal: Lu Jia's rank stopped at grandee; he retired when the Lüs seized power and did not incur the burden of disaster; at ease between Chen Ping and Zhou Bo he harmonized general with chancellor to strengthen the altars of soil and grain—both person and name won glory: is he not the most excellent among them!
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia never rose past grandee, yet he withdrew when the Lüs ruled, dodged ruin, glided between Chen Ping and Zhou Bo, yoked commandant to chancellor to brace the state, and walked off with life and renown intact—surely the soundest judgment of them all!
